response.justification: Sarah Smith is a skilled polyglot engineer with strong TypeScript/Node.js credentials and genuine craftsmanship discipline, but she is a poor match for this role. The position demands deep Java and Kotlin expertise, hands-on Spring/Quarkus/Hibernate experience, and JVM internals knowledge — none of which appear substantively in her work history. Java is listed as a skill and appears briefly at Altom (robotic arms, mobile orchestration) and NI (C-based IDE tooling), but these are peripheral mentions rather than sustained, senior-level JVM work. There is no evidence of Spring ecosystem experience, Kotlin, or any JVM-focused architecture. Her IDE experience at National Instruments is the closest signal but was C/C++ tooling for a non-JVM product, lasted only six months, and was over a decade ago.
strengths: Demonstrated software craftsmanship practices (TDD, CI/CD, trunk-based development) that align with maintaining quality in complex codebases
Prior IDE-adjacent work at National Instruments writing components for a C IDE, showing at least a surface-level familiarity with tooling internals
Experience with high-throughput, large-scale systems (Dunnhumby: millions of HTTP requests/second), indicating comfort with performance-sensitive infrastructure
concerns: No meaningful Java or Kotlin seniority — primary language stack is TypeScript/Node.js; Java appears only as a secondary tool in brief, non-JVM-focused contexts
Zero evidence of Spring, Quarkus, Hibernate, or any enterprise Java framework experience across 15+ years of employment
IDE internals experience is limited to a six-month C/C++ role in 2014–2015, with no JVM IDE work and no apparent follow-through interest in that domain

key_factors.factor: Tangential IDE tooling background (NI, MCP generators)
